verdicts.title: SSRF via $_GET['code'] passed to wp_remote_request - false positive, user input does not control destination URL
verdicts.attack_vector: The taint analysis flagged $_GET['code'] reaching wp_remote_request() at connect.php:87. However, the user-supplied 'code' value is only placed in the POST body of the request ('body' => [..., 'code' => sanitize_text_field(...)]), not in the destination URL. The destination is the hardcoded constant self::API_URL . '/get_token' (https://my.elementor.com/api/connect/v1/get_token). There is no SSRF because the attacker cannot control where the HTTP request goes. Additionally, the handler verifies a WordPress nonce (wp_verify_nonce at lines 49 and 83), validates GET parameters against expected values from Data::get_connect_state() (lines 53-64), and requires an admin page hook ('load-elementor_page_elementor-connect', '_admin_menu'). The sanitize_text_field() call on the code value is sufficient for its role as a POST body parameter being forwarded to the hardcoded Elementor API endpoint.
verdicts.auth_required: admin context (admin page hook; nonce verified)
verdicts.fp_reason: User input only reaches the POST body, not the URL parameter of wp_remote_request(). SSRF requires attacker-controlled URL. The destination is a hardcoded constant.

verdicts.title: RCE via $images_count in array_filter callback - false positive, no shell command execution occurs
verdicts.attack_vector: The taint analysis flagged $images_count flowing into array_filter() at bulk-optimization-token-manager.php:34 as an RCE sink requiring escapeshellarg(). This is entirely wrong: array_filter() with a PHP closure performing a numeric comparison ($size <= $max_batch_size) is not a shell execution function. No shell commands are involved anywhere in this code path. $images_count is declared as a typed int parameter (line 30: int $images_count), so PHP enforces integer type at call boundaries. The only thing that happens to this value is arithmetic (intval($images_count / 2)), inclusion in an integer array, filtering, and passing as an integer to an API request body. The 'missing escapeshellarg()' flag is a scanner artifact - the scanner incorrectly classified array_filter as a command sink.
verdicts.fp_reason: array_filter() with a comparison closure is not a shell execution sink. $images_count is PHP-typed as int. No exec/shell_exec/system/popen/proc_open or equivalent is present in this code path. The scanner mislabeled array_filter as a command injection sink.

summary: Both automated findings for the image-optimization plugin are false positives. The plugin's attack surface is minimal and well-defended: all REST API routes use a permission_callback framework that requires authentication (current_user_id > 0) and the higher-privilege routes require manage_options. The OAuth connect flow double-verifies WordPress nonces before processing any GET parameters. File system operations use the WordPress WP_Filesystem abstraction and the Image_Backup_Path_Validator class enforces that all paths remain within the uploads directory via realpath() and prefix checking. The client library sends image data to hardcoded Elementor API endpoints with no attacker-controllable URL components. No exploitable vulnerabilities were identified in this codebase.
feedback: The taint scanner produced two low-quality findings: (1) it mislabeled the POST body parameter of wp_remote_request as an SSRF source despite the URL being a hardcoded constant; (2) it classified array_filter() as a command execution sink requiring escapeshellarg(), which is incorrect. The scanner's sink taxonomy needs refinement to distinguish URL parameters from body parameters in HTTP client calls, and to exclude PHP array functions from shell-command sink categories. The plugin itself is well-written with consistent auth enforcement, nonce verification, and path traversal protections.
